page_title: Paid family and sick leave — Nevada
audit_score_notes: All block-severity checks passing. Deductions: (1) AB 305 $30 FMLA fee cap sourced from Nevada Employers Association 2026 update only — not independently verified against enrolled bill text. (2) NRS 613.4353 Nevada pregnancy accommodation statute not independently verified against Nevada LCB database. (3) SB 312 amendment check flagged as provisional per task brief instruction — no SB amendments found in primary sources but 2025 session bills not exhaustively reviewed.

flags.detail: AB 305 (2025 session) $30 FMLA certification fee cap, effective 1st January 2026. Source: Nevada Employers Association 2026 update (nevadaemployers.org). Not independently verified against Nevada LCB enrolled bill text before publication. Verify before go-live.
Nevada state pregnancy accommodation obligation under NRS 613.4353 cited per legal reference sources. Verify statute number against Nevada LCB statute database before publication.

Task brief instructs verification against most recent SB amendments to NRS 608.0197. No amendment found in compliance cache (2026-05-30) or primary source review. 2025 Nevada session bills not exhaustively scanned. Verify against Nevada LCB 2025 session chaptered bills before publication.

inline_visual.content: NRS 608.0197 / SB 312 statutory reference — 0.01923 hrs per hr worked, 40 hrs/year, any reason, no documentation. Effective 1st January 2020.
inline_visual.implementation: HTML+CSS stamp-block primitive, consistent with Nevada sibling pages

pullquote.reason: No named individual (Joanna/Paulina/Antony) pullquote required. Anonymised to 'Teamed Legal Operations' per pullquote provenance rules.

editorial_angle: Nevada mandates paid leave for any reason — broader than most US sick-leave states — but has no PFML programme. The any-reason provision is the source of most employer confusion and the page's editorial hook.
